title: Ecosystem Building

content.statement: Build a world, not a product.
content.question: What ecosystem is your product part of, and are you building that, or just your product?
content.life_lens: Life systems that support each other.
sop: List every product, service, or touchpoint you currently offer.
Map which ones reinforce each other versus which stand completely alone.
Identify one adjacent piece (content, community, tool, partnership) that would make your core product harder to leave.
Count your current ecosystem depth, how many interlocking pieces exist today (see calculation, page 6).
Build one new integration point this quarter between two existing offerings.
questions: What ecosystem is your product part of, and are you building that, or just your product?
If a competitor copied your main product tomorrow, what else would they still have to rebuild to actually compete with you?
Which two things you offer would be more valuable used together than either is alone, and do your customers even know that?

definitions.def: A durable competitive advantage that protects your business from competition over time. Like a castle moat, keeps rivals out.

content.equation_example: Ecosystem Depth equals number of interlocking products, services, or community touchpoints that reinforce each other (count). 5 connected pieces (product, content, community, marketplace, support network) gives a Moat score of 5. Higher/rising means a competitor has to rebuild your whole system to compete, not just copy one product; lower/falling means you're one easily-cloned offer.
content.closing_statement: Make competitors rebuild a world, not one product.

caption:
Principle Nº 177: Ecosystem Building

Build a world, not a product.

Ask yourself: What ecosystem is your product part of, and are you building that, or just your product?
